The SilentVoice

A story of redemption & human connection · 2016

Explore the Story
Scroll
"I decided to stop looking at the faces of others. I was afraid of what I might find there."

— Shoya Ishida

Synopsis

When silence speaks louder than words

Shoya Ishida is a troubled young man haunted by the cruelty he inflicted on Shoko Nishimiya, a deaf girl who transferred into his elementary school class. Having bullied her relentlessly until she transferred away, Ishida finds himself ostracized and alone through his school years.

Now in high school, plagued by guilt and social isolation, he sets out to find Shoko and make amends — beginning an unlikely and deeply moving journey toward forgiveness, understanding, and human connection.

Directed by Naoko Yamada, adapted from Yoshitoki Oima's acclaimed manga, this Kyoto Animation masterpiece asks us: can one truly redeem themselves, and can the wounds of the past ever truly heal?

The bridge at Ogaki — where worlds collide

Characters

The souls of the story

Shoya Ishida
石田 将也

The protagonist — once a bully consumed by thrill-seeking, later a young man broken by guilt and isolation, searching desperately for a path to redemption.

Shoko Nishimiya
西宮 硝子

Deaf from birth, Shoko endures cruelty with extraordinary grace. Her quiet resilience and unconditional warmth form the moral center of the entire film.

Yuzuru Nishimiya
西宮 結絃

Shoko's fierce, protective younger sister. Sharp-tongued and deeply loyal, her anger masks an overwhelming love and fear of losing the people she cares about.

Tomohiro Nagatsuka
永束 友宏

Ishida's earnest, endearingly odd friend who pledges undying loyalty with sincere theatricality — a source of warmth and gentle humor in the darkest moments.

Core Themes

What the film asks of us

01

Redemption & guilt

Can a person truly change? The film refuses easy answers. Ishida's journey is painful and non-linear — guilt is not dissolved through grand gestures but through slow, deliberate acts of presence and humility.

02

Disability & empathy

Shoko's deafness is never treated as a deficit to overcome. The film illuminates how society fails those who communicate differently, and how empathy must be actively practiced, not assumed.

03

Isolation & human connection

Both protagonists are profoundly isolated — one by disability, one by shame. Their connection is the film's beating heart: tentative, imperfect, and ultimately transcendent.

04

The weight of memory

Naoko Yamada's visual language makes memory palpable — crossed-out faces, muted color, the weight of eye contact. The past is not past; it lives in posture, in avoidance, in small brave moments of looking up.
